title: Uninformed search

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Uninformed search; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Fundamental Issues and Basic Search Strategies: state spaces, uninformed/informed, and adversarial search; atomic topic: Uninformed search
sources.claim: DFS, BFS, iterative deepening, and uniform-cost search differ in frontier order, duplicate handling, completeness, optimality conditions, time, and memory; selection follows graph and cost assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: DFS, BFS, iterative deepening, and uniform-cost search differ in frontier order, duplicate handling, completeness, optimality conditions, time, and memory; selection follows graph and cost assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: BFS finds a minimum-step route in a finite unweighted graph using a queue and a visited set marked when enqueued. Your goal is to select, implement and instrument an uninformed search for a stated state space. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Agent, goal and utility models; dsa graphs bfs dfs; States, actions, goals and costs; dsa heaps priority queue. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: DFS, BFS, iterative deepening, and uniform-cost search differ in frontier order, duplicate handling, completeness, optimality conditions, time, and memory; selection follows graph and cost assumptions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: BFS is optimal only for equal step costs, DFS can follow infinite/deep branches, uniform-cost needs nonnegative costs, graph search needs a cost-aware visited policy, and practical memory can dominate. The learner must reject this overclaim: BFS always finds the cheapest path and a state should be permanently marked visited the first time any search encounters it.

includes: Compare DFS, BFS, iterative deepening and uniform-cost search by completeness, optimality, time and space.
Select, implement and instrument an uninformed search for a stated state space.
excludes: BFS is optimal only for equal step costs, DFS can follow infinite/deep branches, uniform-cost needs nonnegative costs, graph search needs a cost-aware visited policy, and practical memory can dominate.

outcomes: Select, implement and instrument an uninformed search for a stated state space.

examples.title: Uninformed search: bounded direct case
examples.situation: BFS finds a minimum-step route in a finite unweighted graph using a queue and a visited set marked when enqueued.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: BFS finds a minimum-step route in a finite unweighted graph using a queue and a visited set marked when enqueued.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Match the frontier and duplicate policy to depth and path-cost assumptions; BFS minimizes steps under equal costs, while uniform-cost search minimizes nonnegative accumulated cost.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Match the frontier and duplicate policy to depth and path-cost assumptions; BFS minimizes steps under equal costs, while uniform-cost search minimizes nonnegative accumulated cost.

examples.title: Uninformed search: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A longer route has lower total cost, so uniform-cost search replaces BFS and updates a state when a cheaper path is discovered.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A longer route has lower total cost, so uniform-cost search replaces BFS and updates a state when a cheaper path is discovered.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: BFS is optimal only for equal step costs, DFS can follow infinite/deep branches, uniform-cost needs nonnegative costs, graph search needs a cost-aware visited policy, and practical memory can dominate.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: BFS always finds the cheapest path and a state should be permanently marked visited the first time any search encounters it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Select, implement and instrument an uninformed search for a stated state space.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: BFS always finds the cheapest path and a state should be permanently marked visited the first time any search encounters it.
misconceptions.correction: Match the frontier and duplicate policy to depth and path-cost assumptions; BFS minimizes steps under equal costs, while uniform-cost search minimizes nonnegative accumulated cost.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: BFS always finds the cheapest path and a state should be permanently marked visited the first time any search encounters it.

analogy.example: a route planner that tracks states, legal moves, accumulated cost, estimates, and an ordered frontier can introduce Uninformed search only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: state-space representation, frontier policies, heuristics, and adversarial backup
limitations: real environments can be continuous, stochastic, changing, partially observed, or too large for the simplified route model

recap.summary: You should now be able to select, implement and instrument an uninformed search for a stated state space. Keep this boundary in view: BFS is optimal only for equal step costs, DFS can follow infinite/deep branches, uniform-cost needs nonnegative costs, graph search needs a cost-aware visited policy, and practical memory can dominate.
